data.title: Federal Government Efficiency Commission Trickle-Down To State And Local Municipalities
data.description:
By Patricia Healy, CFA

Elon Musk was in the news stumping for Donald Trump and bringing attention to his petition supporting guns and free speech, by offering registered voters the daily chance to win $1 million by reading and signing the petition. While $1 million grabs headlines (and may run afoul of election laws), the article is mostly about Musk making the case to lead a proposed Department of Government Efficiency or D.O.G.E., or at least to spotlight the need for efficiency.

Government efficiency is always a discussion point - for example, there is the $600 the Navy spent for a hammer in the 1980s, as well as the increasing cost of building projects and pipelines. Delving into these examples shows the complex environment that the military operates in and the hurdles that project developers must jump. No matter which candidate wins the presidential election, the need to remove government waste and over-regulation will continue to be discussed, as will spending priorities.

Federal dollars flow to state and local governments as well as to individuals and businesses in many ways from direct payments, grants, subsidies, and tax credits; and we are always keen to assess how federal spending and regulatory changes may affect money flowing to municipalities. For example, pandemic funding is coming to an end, and many are looking at using reserves, raising taxes, cutting expenses, and issuing debt.

The heart of the efficiency push is not to bring fewer dollars to state and local governments, but to reduce over-regulation of projects and remove impediments to efficient operation. Philip K. Howard, chairman of Common Good, has for years promoted the idea of creating a less regulated operating environment and has recommended ways to address the process. He also discusses how regulation stifles creative thought and removes personal responsibility.

Both Musk and Howard point to the over-regulation, especially regarding infrastructure development, that has been delaying projects and making them more costly. As we have mentioned in the past, the Association of Civil Engineers Report Card on US infrastructure, conducted every four years, showed an overall grade of C- in 2021, indicating mediocre condition, although it had improved from a grade of D in 2017 and for the previous 20 years. The 2025 infrastructure report card is expected in March 2025.

The devastation of our infrastructure by natural disasters, especially the damage in western North Carolina from flooding due to Hurricane Helene, has brought attention especially to our water infrastructure. The report card for drinking water also had an improved grade of C- in 2021. In addition to maintaining, replacing, expanding, and improving our drinking water infrastructure, it is important to remove contaminants to provide safe drinking water, which generally adds cost, so it is important to be efficient. The infrastructure and Jobs Act did provide funding for water and sewer and other infrastructure improvements, so it will be informative to see what the next ASCE report card shows!

The process of improving efficiency in government and in setting or resetting regulations may not proceed quickly, given the myriad constituents involved. This may provide lead time for state and local governments, businesses, and individuals to prepare for change. At Cumberland Advisors we think high-quality munis like those we invest in with an average rating of AA, supported by diverse economic bases, improved budgeting and financial management, and ample reserves, will be able to accommodate changes in the availability of federal dollars as well as changing legislation and regulations.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Sonos: A Failed App Rollout Is Creating Tough Times
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I can get behind Sonos (NASDAQ:SONO) as a company; however, I have concerns about their current strategy for product rollout, development, and engagement with customers. Sonos has been held to a standard of best-in-class products, in which they target an affluent customer. However, in 2024, Sonos fell below this standard due to its poorly executed app rollout. I think Sonos will have to re-establish its ethos and credibility in the marketplace, which will take time to recover.

Business Overview

Sonos develops multi-room, wireless audio products, and sound systems. The product lineup includes wireless, portable, and home theater speakers, and headphones. The Sonos platform offers a variety of providers including the likes of Spotify, Apple Music, Pandora, and partnerships with car manufacturers such as Audi. In 2023 Sonos' revenue surpassed $1.6bn from their 22 different product offerings. Sonos currently distributes to over 60 countries and has filed over 3800+ patent applications (1,500 are issued) for their proprietary technology. Its main differentiation factors are the combination of an open content and control platform with hardware that spans different use cases and price points.

Earnings

In Q3 Sonos reported strong results with revenue and EBITDA beating estimates. Sales increased 6% YoY. However, the company experienced significant backlash from their failed rollout of the app. Gross margin in Q3 was 46.4% and an adjusted EBITDA of $155mm, representing a 12.3% margin. These bugs have proven to be very difficult to fix, damaging Sonos’ reputation. This negativity overshadowed the successful launch of the brand’s Ace headphones. In the near term, their Q4 earnings are going to feel the effects, with sales estimated to decline 20% YoY compared to an increase of 30% YoY. Additionally, there are concerns as to whether retailers will leave Sonos out of key holiday merchandising strategies if these issues cannot be resolved.

Looking at guidance for Q4, gross margins are expected to come in at 40% resulting from the lower revenue from the backlash. Q4 EBITDA is expected to take a large hit too with a -$37 to -$14mm range representing a 6.2% to 7.7% margin, again correlated to the decrease in revenue.

Economics of the New Sonos App

The major question that has to be asked and clarified is, what was wrong with the Sonos app? The app was meant to address performance and reliability issues that the company had been experiencing over the last 2 decades, which were affecting customer experience. The app was deemed essential to the growth of Sonos and a lever to expand into new categories. Patrick Spence, CEO of Sonos, noted in the Q3 earnings call that the rollout of the app “was to be a modular developer platform based on modern programming languages that will allow Sonos to drive more innovation faster, and thus let Sonos deliver all kinds of new features over time that the old app simply couldn’t accommodate.” In my view, Sonos rushed the rollout of their new app in anticipation of the launch of the Ace headphones. If there was no app, Ace wouldn’t have functionality, and this feeling of pressure caused Sonos to overlook some of the vital pieces to its ethos as a brand.

Looking at it from a technical perspective, Sonos devices have to connect to their proprietary wireless network for the products to have functionality. The new app switched from SSDP (the previous app) to mDNS for discovering speakers on the network. This change caused widespread issues with the app being unable to pair with devices. There was also significant performance degradation as it was significantly slower due to encryption and a switch from UPnP (universal plug and play) to Sonos’ cloud API. An increase in network traffic ultimately led to lag for users.

Direct customer feedback was quite harsh, rightfully so, and those issues were more table stakes than anything. Users experienced volume control issues, as pairing speakers wouldn’t work in unison. The delays and inconsistencies were caused by the app using web sockets rather than UPnP events, which compromised the responsiveness of the system. Customers reported that the new UI was difficult to use. Sonos built its brand on functionality and ease of use, but that has drifted here. Initially, at the app launch, there were several key features notably missing, which included the queue management from the previous version that users enjoyed. It appears that Sonos was borderline inept, and many of these issues should have been addressed in some sort of beta testing.

Sonos’ hardware is excellent, but the products are reliant on the app. Without the app there is no utility, you can’t stream audio, reassign speakers, tune them, or adjust the configuration.

The Road Ahead

Sonos has a steep hill to climb and trying to restore the company’s reputation. Spence stated that fixing the app is the main priority, and they will be issuing programs to thank customers and partners for sticking with them through this period. This will take place during Q4 and into Q1 2025.

For Sonos, I believe it is going to get worse before it gets better. However, there must be a path forward. The three main takeaways from the Q3 earnings call that must be addressed are:

1. Although there is short-term pain, what are the growth prospects long term, and how does Sonos get there?

2. How do the app issues affect purchase intent moving forward?

3. What is Sonos’ plan to win back loyal and new customers’ trust?

Management emphasized the need to focus on long-term growth, aiming to increase their current market share of less than 2% in the $100 billion audio market. Their primary goals are regaining consumer trust and improving the performance of the existing app. Sonos lacked execution with the app, however, if they can address any issues, the flywheel can continue to prosper into the future. Management indicated that they will invest $20-30mm dedicated to resolving existing issues, which should help restore reliability and trust while enabling them to continue delivering the high-quality products they have been recognized for.

Potential Upside Case

Considering Sonos’ patent portfolio of over 3,800 they have incredible defensibility around their IP, providing a moat fostering innovation while protecting technological advancements.

Sonos is building a product ecosystem that is robust, similar to Apple. The current installed base is continuing to purchase additional Sonos products, which can have a great impact on their future revenue.

Sonos' brand and market position are too valuable to remain in this bargain bucket for an extended period. The company partners and installer channels will be back on board if Sonos can continue to introduce innovative products. Although short-term, this is not the case. There is value here looking at a long-term outlook and the patient investors.

Valuation

I found my cost of equity using the CAPM model accompanied by the 30-year treasury bill as my risk-free rate and added it to the beta of 1.65. I multiplied it by the equity risk premium, which is the TTM S&P 500 return subtracted from my 30-year treasury bill risk-free rate.

The cost of debt of 5% was pulled from Bloomberg.

To calculate my WACC, I multiplied them by the % of equity and % of debt in the capital structure to arrive at 11.2% as my discount rate.

My revenue projections are more conservative than consensus. I think that Sonos will struggle to regain its market presence in the near term, and customers will scoff at the recent problems the company has had. There is little faith in the market right now, as Sonos currently trades at a 6x EV/EBITDA (FY 2025). I wanted to model this on a more short-term basis and chose a 7x EV/EBITDA multiple compared to consensus at 10.5x as there is uncertainty for what lies ahead for Sonos. I believe there will be an increase in working capital as the company has to continue to spend on R&D to innovate new products, which is a main driver of revenue. This build was very sensitive to my inputs, however, that can change for the better if the company starts to regain traction again.

Conclusion

While I am not buying Sonos now, it is something I will be closely monitoring over the next 2 quarters as investors will get a better sense of where the company stands towards the end of Q1. I have some belief in the long-term vision of Sonos and where the company is headed, but I am not convinced by management at this current stage. I’d like to see these plans rolled out and evidence that Sonos is beginning to turn the corner.

data.title: Amazon: Watch Out For A Single Number
data.description:
Amazon’s (NASDAQ:AMZN) most important metric for the upcoming quarter is likely going to be the YoY advertising growth rate. Wall Street is becoming increasingly concerned over the massive investments made in AI chips. The quickest way to show good returns on these investments is through better AI tools in advertising. Meta (META) reported 22% YoY growth in its advertising revenue in Q2 2024 which was higher than Google’s (GOOG) YoY advertising growth of 11% and higher than Amazon’s 20% growth in advertising business. This helped Meta stock deliver a strong bullish trajectory since the last earnings. Total returns in Meta stock since the last earnings is 21%, compared to 3% for Amazon stock and negative 7% for Alphabet stock. In the previous article, it was mentioned that Amazon could deliver margin expansion through AWS and cost-cutting measures.

The long-term growth story is important for Amazon, but it would need to show good results in key metrics in the short term to boost the stock sentiment. It should be noted that Amazon’s AWS has seen good growth and margin numbers in the last four quarters. The trailing twelve-month operating margin of AWS has increased from 25.8% in Q3 2023 to 33.4% in Q2 2024. Amazon is also investing heavily in automation and robotics within its warehouse and distribution network. This should help the company build a strong moat and gain good margin expansion over the next few quarters.

Amazon’s EPS estimate for the fiscal year ending 2026 is $7.3 which gives the stock a forward P/E ratio of 25 for fiscal year 2026. This is lower than Apple’s (AAPL) forward P/E ratio of 28 and close to Microsoft’s (MSFT) 24 for fiscal year 2026. We can see that Amazon stock is not very expensive when compared with other peers. It is possible to see EPS upward revisions if Amazon’s AI investment help improve the margins in AWS and advertising segment.

Focus on advertising growth rate

Amazon’s advertising segment has broken the digital advertising duopoly of Meta and Google. In a recent report, eMarketer mentioned that Amazon’s advertising market share could soon reach close to that of Meta and Google. This would be a great achievement for the company, which had very low revenue in this segment a few years back.

Figure: Amazon’s advertising market share growth. Source: eMarketer

Advertising is a high-margin business, and strong revenue growth in this segment could improve the overall margins for Amazon. Wall Street is also giving greater importance to margins and EPS among the big tech companies as the AI hype decreases. After the Q2 2024 results, Meta stock has been the best performing. A big reason is the strong 22% YoY growth in its advertising revenue. On the other hand, Alphabet reported only 11% YoY growth in its advertising revenue and Amazon reported 20% growth in advertising segment.

Figure: Meta’s outperformance compared to Amazon and Google. Source: Ycharts

Figure: Decline in Amazon’s advertising growth rate in the last few quarters. Source: Amazon Filings

Over the last few quarters, Amazon’s advertising segment has seen a slight decrease in YoY growth rate, falling from 26% in Q4 2023 to 20% in Q2 2024. Amazon is building new AI-based tools for advertisers which should help in improving the YoY growth trajectory within the advertising business. Amazon will also be launching ads on Prime Video within important markets like Japan, Brazil and India in 2025. This should be another tailwind for the advertising business.

Good growth in AWS and automation

Amazon’s AWS is showing good growth inflection over the last few quarters. The YoY revenue growth in AWS increased from 12% in Q3 2023 to 19% in Q2 2024. There has also been a strong expansion in operating margin. The TTM operating margin in AWS has increased from 25.8% in Q3 2023 to 33.4% in Q2 2024. This has helped improve the company's overall margins. AWS still contributes over 60% of the total operating margin for the company. If this growth trend continues, we could see better EPS numbers from the company in the near term.

Figure: Key metrics for AWS in the last few quarters. Source: Amazon Filings

Another major tailwind for Amazon will be the automation within its warehouses and shipping network. The company has invested heavily in robotics to reduce labor costs over the last few years. These investments will finally start showing results in the near term. The cost-cutting drive announced by the management should also help in improving the margins.

Figure: Growth in revenue and total employees of Amazon in last few years. Source: Ycharts

We can see in the above chart that Amazon’s headcount growth has reduced significantly in the last few quarters, while the revenue growth has been quite strong. This has helped improve the margins significantly.

Is the stock pricey?

Amazon’s market cap is close to $2 trillion, and any investment at the current price will need to closely look at the growth runway for the company. Unlike many other big tech companies, Amazon currently does not give dividends or invest in buybacks, which increases the importance of stock price growth. There was a small buyback program in 2022 but it was soon discontinued. The management is focusing on investing resources in new initiatives and logistics instead of having a big capital return program.

We can look at the improvement in valuation multiple over the last few quarters.

Figure: Amazon's valuation multiple. Source: Ycharts

Amazon's P/E ratio has come down sharply in the last few quarters due to rapid EPS growth. The 3-year median of P/E ratio for Amazon was 67 while the forward P/E ratio is 32. As shown below, there is a strong EPS growth projection for the next few quarters, which should further reduce the forward P/E ratio.

Figure: Forward EPS projection of Amazon. Source: Seeking Alpha

Over the next few quarters, Amazon is projected to continue improving its EPS as more revenue comes from higher margin businesses like AWS and advertising compared to its e-commerce segment. For the fiscal year ending Dec 2026, Amazon’s EPS is estimated to be $7.3 and the forward P/E is expected to be 25.89. This is still quite reasonable compared to other tech peers.

Figure: Comparison of forward EPS of Amazon and other big tech companies. Source: Ycharts

Apple’s forward P/E ratio for fiscal year 2026 is 28, Microsoft is 24, and Nvidia (NVDA) is 30. Compared to these big tech companies, Amazon’s forward P/E ratio of 25 for fiscal year 2026 looks quite reasonable. The YoY revenue growth projection of Amazon is also quite robust as the company continues to invest heavily in international operations and builds a more robust logistics network.

Amazon has a good moat and if the company delivers strong advertising growth in the upcoming earnings, we could see a bullish momentum for the stock.

Investor Takeaway

Amazon’s upcoming quarter and the near-term stock direction will be highly dependent on the performance of the advertising segment. We have already seen Meta outperform all other peers due to its strong advertising growth. The AWS segment continues to perform well and is showing margin expansion despite the massive revenue base.

Amazon stock does not seem to be very pricey compared to other big tech peers when we look at forward EPS growth estimates. Slower growth in the low-margin e-commerce segment will also help increase the revenue share of higher-margin segments. This should help in improving the EPS growth trajectory over the next few quarters. The management has taken a number of steps to reduce costs and optimize headcount. The upside potential for the stock is quite good, making it a Buy at the current price.

data.title: Engie: Finding More Value In France With A 'Buy'
data.description:
Dear readers/followers,

In this article, I will be doing my first specific review of the multi-utility/utility company Engie SA (OTCPK:ENGIY). My foray into French investments has continued this year, and I have significantly increased my overall exposure to this stock market over 2023-2024. I also have no intention of cutting or lowering my exposure here - the opposite, as a matter of fact. I view the companies, provided their valuation works, as appealing given typical yield patterns and the current valuation state of the entire French market.

Engie S.A. is not an entirely uncomplicated business. But over the last 24 months, the company has worked to derisk its entire business in quite a spectacular fashion. The trading and valuation patterns reflect a company that has a long history of lackluster performance behind it. But Engie, as of this time, gives us not only a solid yield but a potential upside worth considering.

Why do I like these companies and the upsides they offer?

Because during lower interest rates, utilities like Engie will become more attractive due to what they offer. Wholesale power prices in Europe may have stabilized and government bond yields are falling on weak economic indicators, but this company is moving upward- and the trajectory since COVID-19 confirms it.

So let's look at what Engie is and what it offers.

Engie - Upside and fair value show us that this company may be an attractive investment.

Engie is one of the largest, fully integrated European utilities. The only companies that can come close to delivering the same are Enel (OTCPK:ENLAY) and Iberdrola (OTCPK:IBDRY) - and if you follow my work, you'll note that I actually invest in these businesses. Engie is a company that I have bought what I would consider a "watchlist" position, meaning a very minimal €1000 investment to keep an eye on where it's going. And things have been going well.

This is a huge company. The company supplies electricity to 27 nations in Europe, and 48 nations in the world. It was formed in the early days of the GFC by the merger of Gaz de France, a very traditional company, and Suez, which in turn can show history to 1858 when the Suez Canal was built.

Engie employs nearly 100,000 people and generates revenues upward of €100B. Again, one of the largest utilities on the planet. It trades under the native symbol ENGI on the French stock market.

Like most other utilities, Engie has moved from Fossil fuels to heavy investments into renewables, in this case, things like solar, wind, geothermal, biomass generation, hydroelectric power, and nuclear power. In addition to this, heavy investments have been made into efficiency and energy services. In the last 10 years, the company has made it clear that no further investments in coal will be made and in 2015 Engie announced €22B in renewable investments. In 2016, the company adopted a new strategy, and the 2019 strategic plan has been worked on since that time. The company has left nations and areas behind, with a future focus on 20 countries and 30 metropolitan areas, mainly in Southeast Asia and Africa, which the company views as profitable geographies.

The latest results as of this time are 2Q24, with 3Q24 just around the corner and able to be forecasted and worked with here.

The company saw a good EBIT, with a normalization sort of quarter - some KPIs were still down, but not down as much, and there were signs of a back to normal. This is also the reason why the company upgraded it's 2024 guidance. EBIT came in at €5.6B for the half-year, a run rate of over €11B for 2024A, and a CFFO of €8.9B or a run rate of close to €20B for the year. Engie has been higher-leveraged than many of its peers for some time, and the company's economic net debt is now below €46B, down almost €800M since the 1H24 segment - again, an improvement here.

As you can see, Engie is on track to deliver significant investments into ESG and in its renewable portfolio. There are plenty of examples out there with regard to how Engie is working, such as Texas. Texas is an energy market that is in dire need of what is called flex generation, and the company's assets are on par with delivering this for the future.

The company has built over 800 MW of BRP batteries in 1H24 alone, and these builds were completed on time, with hedged cash flows on average at a hedging level of 50% until 2029.

The fact that EBIT is down should be taken in the context that EBIT is down from a record high. This is driving down everything on a comparative basis. Nuclear EBIT and other renewable EBIT, on the other hand, is up - Nuclear is up over €200M in a single half-year YoY and is continuing to push upwards. The company's renewable segment now generates over 20% of the company's quarterly and half-year EBIT levels.

On a segment basis, the company operates the relatively self-explanatory segments of Renewables, Networks, Energo Solutions (like on-site energy products, energy production, and local energy networks), Flex Gen (aforementioned, with areas in Chile and NA as well as LATAM, but mostly EU), Retail and Nuclear.

Engie's savings program is on track to deliver more savings as well, upwards of €200M per year. The entire quarter can in fact be summarized by the company having quite an excellent sort of progress.

About a year ago, the company came to an agreement with the Belgian government for a 10-year extension for Belgian nuclear assets - this has also been positive in this quarter, including a €4.5B waste increase in provisions, and this will be transferred to the Belgian government in exchange for €16B in payments by Engie to fund these reactors and things in 2024-2025. What this means is that Engie effectively has no more exposure to these cost evolutions. This is part of what we talk about when we're talking about "derisking Engie" - it really is a risk reduction in terms of equity.

With significant renewable non-nuclear assets coming online, the company is only behind Iberdrola and Enel, and not by that, much anymore given that it targets much more installation going forward as well. Engie is different than Enel and Iberdrola here, as these two companies have moved into investing in networks instead, given the poor returns for asset investments in renewables at this time, among other things.

The uncertain part here is, as I see it, the dividend. Engie's backward-looking dividend yield is part of an earnings story that no longer exists. Engie targets a 65-75% payout in terms of EPS - and the profit normalization Engie is currently expecting and will continue to expect is likely to reduce this more.

The best we can expect is for the company to apply the upper range of the payout ratio.

This makes this an "expectation" sort of game.

The positives of Engie are fairly understood. The company is well-diversified and this asset diversification will work in its favor as it benefits from commodity volatilities and geographical spreads. The eventual closure of nuclear alongside the aforementioned deals de-risks the company considerably.

But at the same time, the company has risks due to its limited exposure to actual electricity networks. It has French gas infrastructure, and we'll see what value this ends up having given that we're seeing an energy transition. Like many French major companies, the government of France also has a significant voting right in this business - 33.8% here, which is not a majority but close to it - and when it's a government that has exposure to politics, this can distort interests with other shareholders. These are the primary risks to Engie - aside from the company's growth in earnings, like for almost all utilities, being entirely dependent on investment returns depending on massive competition and the like.

So let's look at the valuation dictated for the company here.

Valuation for Engie - Attractive, but tricky due to the estimated earnings decline for the next few years.

My view on the company valuation is as follows.

Engie typically trades at around 13.25x P/E. The current P/E is around 7.24x. But this is significantly impacted by higher-than-usual earnings for the past few years, with earnings normalization being forecasted by every single analyst I know and follow. The only difference is the level, and where this forecast puts the company. Also, what sort of P/E should be used to forecast Engie in a declining earnings environment?

Engie is BBB+ rated, and according to the forecasts for the dividend today, has a yield that is over 6% - though this is in part because of the company's tradition to boost its dividend with bonus payouts. But a 75% payout dictates that even on a 2026E basis a dividend payout of around €1.14, which comes to around 7% yield on the basis of the current native forecast in EPS for the ENGI symbol in Paris (Source: F.A.S.T Graphs Paywalled Link).

This is an attractive yield - but we also need to expect that declining EPS and the valuation we apply here.

My fair value calculation is based on an essentially flat, or at most 0.6% EBITDA growth and a negative decline at 3-4% per year in EPS at least until 2026 due to normalization in energy management and sales profit, as well as power prices. I work with a midcycle assumption of €50-€60 per mWh. Any savings the company gets from Belgium and the closures there will be nothing next to new investments into renewable assets. I also expect the decline will be made worse by rising financing costs and D&A, because of the real-leveraging planned by Engie in combination with the aforementioned investments. The calculation here is a WACC of 6.9% with an 8.9% cost of equity with a peer average RoE for a diversified utility of this size.

What this means in raw numbers is that I forecast at most a 15% annualized return at a price up to from today and €16.2/share. Anything above €16.2/share is when I see that RoR potentially go down below 15%; ergo, the company is not a "Buy" at such a valuation above this.

I will say the shares are undervalued here, but I don't see them as undervalued as some others do. Some analyst averages are up to €18/share (Source: S&P Global, SeekingAlpha/TIKR.com), and Morningstar is around there as well. I work with a more conservative estimate because I have a higher hurdle.

My thesis for Engie is therefore as follows, but I work with a "Buy" here, and I own the stock.

Thesis

  • Engie is, together with Iberdrola and Enel, one of the more interesting central European utilities in existence. It's an asset-heavy investor as opposed to a networks-heavy investor at this particular time, and compared to the other two companies. It's a €100B+ company with operations across the world, and it pays a well-covered dividend.
  • However, it's derisking strategy and earnings profile have left it exposed to an earnings normalization story likely to play out over the next 3-4 years. This means that any investment made needs to consider seriously the potential of lower dividends. I estimate a 2026E payout of €1.14, coming to a current yield of 7.3% - which is quite excellent - but might also be lower given the company's overall track record.
  • I give the company a very careful "Buy" here but one with a fairly minimal upside, only to a €16.2/share Fair value estimate. I bought Engie when it was cheaper, and would love to buy it below €14/share. But it's still a "Buy" here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfils them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfils 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. Based on this, and a valuation target of €16.2/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a "Buy" rating as of October 2024.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: XYLD: A Solid Income Play Or Missed Growth?
data.description:
The Global X S&P 500 Covered Call ETF (NYSEARCA:XYLD) has been a popular choice among investors looking to generate consistent income, especially in volatile markets. By employing a covered call or "buy-write" strategy on the S&P 500 Index (SPY), the ETF provides regular income through option premiums. However, opinions on XYLD's long-term performance and its ability to weather different market conditions vary, with critics pointing to several key challenges in its structure.

In this article, we will explore XYLD’s investment approach, performance history, and how it compares to alternative covered call funds, using insights from both primary input sources and the Global X website.

Investment Strategy

At its core, XYLD follows a covered call strategy. This means the fund buys all the stocks in the S&P 500 Index and simultaneously writes call options on the index. According to the Global X website, the goal is to generate income through the premiums received from selling the options, while maintaining exposure to the performance of the S&P 500.

One of the primary benefits of this strategy is that it allows investors to earn income even in relatively flat or slightly declining markets. The collected premiums provide a buffer against market drops, which is particularly appealing for income-seeking investors in a volatile environment. This defensive nature is evident in XYLD's performance during market downturns. For example, in 2022, the S&P 500 fell by 18%, while XYLD only lost 12%, largely due to the premiums it collected from writing call options.

However, this strategy also has limitations. XYLD systematically sells call options with a one-month tenor, which caps its potential upside in a rising market. When the S&P 500 performs well, XYLD underperforms due to the ceiling created by its sold options. In essence, investors sacrifice the potential for substantial gains in exchange for regular income. This tradeoff becomes more pronounced during bullish periods, where funds that don't employ capped strategies, or use more active management, tend to outperform.

Investors should ask themselves whether they are willing to give up the upside potential of a standard long position in the S&P 500 in exchange for option income.

Comparing XYLD to Actively Managed Funds

One significant criticism of XYLD is its systematic approach to selling one-month options, which leads to underperformance compared to actively managed covered call funds. Actively managed funds like the Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Opportunities Fund (ETV) or the Eaton Vance Enhanced Equity Income Fund (EOI) have more flexibility in terms of the duration and timing of option sales. These funds tend to perform better in bullish markets by monetizing short-term trends and reacting to market events, which XYLD's systematic strategy cannot do​.

A recent comparison of XYLD with newer funds like the Roundhill S&P 500 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (XDTE), which utilizes zero-day-to-expiration (0DTE) options, further highlights the weaknesses in XYLD’s strategy. XDTE has shown better results since its inception by dynamically adjusting its option sales, capturing short-term volatility more effectively than XYLD's rigid one-month approach.

We should point out the comparison cannot be done one-on-one as XDTE uses a different strategy, but still the difference in performance during the past 6 months was quite large, as XDTE almost delivered 2x the ROI of XYLD.

Historical Performance

XYLD’s long-term performance showcases its income-generating capability but also highlights its limitations. Since its inception in 2013, XYLD has trailed the S&P 500 in total return. This underperformance is largely due to the cap on upside gains imposed by its covered call strategy.

In the past the 10 years, XYLD would have delivered "only" 80% in ROI to its investors, whereas the S&P 500 delivered almost 250% over this same period. That's the consequence of systematically exchanging the upside potential against received call option premiums.

The fund has generated a total return of approximately 20% over the past year, compared to the S&P 500's 40% return over the same period​.

This stark difference in performance shows that XYLD is better suited for investors with a neutral or bearish outlook on the market. In a scenario where the S&P 500 continues to rise, as it has in recent years, XYLD will continue to underperform, as its strategy forces it to give up potential upside in exchange for option premiums. On the flip side, XYLD’s defensive characteristics make it a solid choice during periods of market downturn or volatility (as we have seen for example in 2022).

Income Generation and Risk Factors

XYLD's main appeal is its ability to provide a steady source of income through monthly distributions. According to the Global X website, XYLD has historically delivered an attractive yield, which can be particularly beneficial for retirees or income-focused investors. The current distribution yield stands at 9.44%, a significant figure in the current market environment. This high yield is largely driven by the elevated premiums available in today’s volatile market, where options are priced more generously.

Despite this attractive income, investors should be aware of the risks involved. XYLD still retains exposure to most of the downside risk in the market. While the premiums collected from option sales provide some cushion, they do not fully protect against large market declines. This was evident during bear markets like 2020 and 2021, where XYLD suffered significant drawdowns. The income from options may help to offset these losses, but they do not eliminate the risk entirely​.

One of the key challenges with covered call strategies like XYLD is that they are less effective in fast-rising markets. In a prolonged bull market, the fund’s upside is capped, meaning that it will lag behind the broader S&P 500 Index and potentially other actively managed funds that are better positioned to capture market gains. This is particularly important for investors with a long-term investment horizon, where growth is critical to meet financial goals​.

Another risk we need to take into consideration is the overall underlying implied volatility level of the S&P 500 Index. The total gain from the covered call positions will be directly tied to the value of the sold call options, which is impacted by the underlying implied volatility of the S&P 500 index.

From the above chart, we can see the VIX is currently at the 18.5 level, which is relatively high in comparison with the levels we have seen earlier this year. A decline in volatility would likely reduce the income generated by XYLD's written call option strategy.

Conclusion

XYLD remains a decent option for investors seeking consistent income and lower volatility, especially in a market downturn or sideways trend. Its covered call strategy provides regular income through premiums, making it attractive for retirees or income-focused investors who prioritize stability over growth. However, the fund’s systematic approach to selling one-month options limits its potential upside in a rising market, causing it to underperform compared to the broader S&P 500 and actively managed funds like EOI, ETV, and XDTE.

For investors with a neutral or bearish outlook on the market, or those focused primarily on income, XYLD offers an appealing risk/reward profile. However, for those expecting continued growth in the S&P 500, there are better alternatives in the buy-write space. Investors should carefully consider their market outlook and income needs before choosing XYLD or exploring other, more flexible options available in the market today.

In my personal view, I would prefer not to miss out on the potential of the S&P500 by systematically writing calls on it. The recent bull market shows us how much this can cost an investor in missed earnings potential.

data.title: Installed Building Products: Rising Institutional Pessimism Despite Strong Market Tailwinds
data.description:
Installed Building Products, Inc. (NYSE:IBP) is one week ahead of releasing Q3 2024 earnings results.

Since the stock price surged by 145% during the bull run from October 2023 to March 2024, it has since been moving sideways, showing a slowdown in momentum and an increase in pessimism, particularly among institutional investors, with a 96% increase in the put/call ratio in the last quarter.

Considering the nature of its business, IBP's growth trajectory remains closely tied to the residential construction industry, particularly new single-family home starts.

I believe that market conditions will likely remain favorable in 2025, as further interest rate cuts seem highly likely. This could lead to lower mortgage rates and an increase in buyer confidence.

In this article, I will discuss the rationale behind my Buy rating, including a deep dive into the fundamentals of the company, an overview of insider and institutional activity, and a simple valuation analysis.

For now, I'd like to start with a brief company overview section for those readers new to this stock.

Company Overview

IBP is an installer of insulation and complementary building products. They operate through a national network of over 250 branch locations across the United States.

They mainly provide installation services for residential new construction, as well as repair, remodeling, and commercial building markets.

Their products include insulation, fireproofing, fire stopping, garage doors, waterproofing, rain gutters, window blinds, and other similar products.

The company operates in one business segment, however the latest 10Q provides a breakdown by end market and product.

Ownership-wise, I am glad to see a 16.5% ownership of common stock among all 14 directors and executive officers.

Price Action

The daily chart below shows a 120% increase in the share price since October 2023.

However, there has been a slowdown this year, with the price trading sideways (although with large swings) since February 2024, with the RSI cooling off in the daily chart.

A comparison with the residential construction industry, the consumer cyclical sector, and the S&P 500 shows IBP outperforming these benchmarks in the last 2 years.

Let's dive deep into the fundamentals and understand what drove this increase in the share price.

Fundamentals

As mentioned in the company overview section, IBP's performance is closely tied to new home construction, especially single-family homes, which make up the majority of their business.

I considered including below a table with the new single-family unit starts per quarter to better understand the bull run in Q4 2023 and Q1 2024.

As you can see, there has been a notable YoY increase in the number of new single-family home starts in both Q4 2023 and Q1 2024.

Therefore, I was not surprised to see revenue and gross profit improving in these two quarters.

Operating income also improved, in the double digits, however, net income declined in Q4 2023, despite the tailwinds in new home construction.

Looking at the figures from the last quarter, the company has maintained its growth trajectory, with revenue, operating income, and net income growing in the mid to high single digits.

Moving on to the balance sheet, the company managed to reduce its net debt in the last year, sitting at $591 million at the end of Q2 2024.

As seen in the table below, cash per share has increased by 50% YoY. Additionally, I am encouraged to see positive tangible book value, which has been improving consistently since Q1 2023, mainly driven by the improvement in cash & equivalents.

As a result of the cash improvements, the liquidity ratios look quite decent.

Ratio Value
Current Ratio 3.08
Quick Ratio 2.59
Cash Ratio 1.08

Debt levels seem quite manageable, with $844.0 million in long-term debt remaining after accounting for current maturities.

Speaking of maturities, the nearest one is in April 2025. This is a note payable of $0.6 million at 5% interest, which is easily manageable considering the $380.3 million cash & equivalents.

Additionally, the company holds a term loan of $494.3 million, refinanced in March 2024 with a new maturity date of March 2031, and $297 million in senior notes due 2028.

Moving on to the cash flow statement, I have to admit that I am encouraged by the double-digit YoY growth in operating and free cash flows.

Aside from the improvements in net income discussed above, there were several non-cash adjustments that impacted in a favorable way operating cash flow in the first half of this year.

Among them, $28.0 million in depreciation and amortization expenses related to property and equipment, $16.3 million in amortization expenses related to the right-of-use assets under operating leases, $21.2 million in amortization of intangibles, and $3.1 million in provision for credit losses.

It seems that the company has been very effective at generating cash from operations, benefiting from the rise in new single-family home starts since the end of 2023.

The outlook for single-family starts in 2025 looks favorable, with Fannie Mae projecting mortgage originations for single-family homes to grow from $1.98 trillion in 2024 to $2.44 trillion in 2025, despite the lack of confidence of residential builders in 2024.

Insider / Activist / Institutional Activity

I remain disappointed with the lack of insider buying activity in the last 2 years. Additionally, in May and March this year, several insiders sold a significant amount of their ownership, including the COO, Wheeler Brad, who sold $273k worth of shares.

Looking at activist activity, there hasn't been anything significant since the beginning of the year.

Institutional investors seem to be more pessimistic, with a 96% increase in the put/call ratio in Q2 2024. Additionally, the number of closed positions increased by 47% in the same quarter.

Considering the valuation ratios of the company (see next section), I am not surprised by the pessimism among institutional investors.

Valuation

A quick look at the chart below shows most valuation ratios trading close to the 5-year maximum.

There is a notable exception for the P/CF ratio, considering the improvement in operating cash flows in the last year.

Let’s compare these ratios to those of their industry peers to see if they are on the higher side.

As a side note, the table above was sorted in descending order by EV/EBITDA. IBP is the 4th most expensive company in the group if we use this metric for comparison.

You can find the results below if we use P/CF as a comparison metric.

In my view, despite most of these ratios trading close to their 5-year maximum, I don't think the company is overvalued when compared to its peers.

Risks

In my opinion, one of the biggest risks to my optimistic outlook is a delay in interest rate cuts, which could keep mortgage costs high and reduce demand for new homes.

Given the company's reliance on new single-family home construction, any slowdown in housing starts would directly impact its revenue.

Additionally, IBP's valuation might appear high compared to its historical levels, making the stock more susceptible to broader market corrections. In my view, if institutional pessimism persists despite interest rate cuts, as signaled by the rising put/call ratio and increased closed positions, the stock could experience further downside.

Conclusion

To wrap up, I hold a Buy rating for IBP, as the company’s strong fundamentals are backed by positive industry trends that are expected to continue into next year.

I am encouraged by the consistent increase (YoY) in new single-family home starts in the last 3 quarters. Additionally, further interest rate cuts could lead to mortgage rates cooling off in 2025, improving buyer's confidence in new single-family units.

Additionally, the company’s balance sheet improvements, including higher cash reserves and manageable debt levels, provide a solid financial foundation if there is a slowdown in new home starts next year.

However, I must consider some risks. The recent slowdown in share price momentum and insider selling activity is concerning. Valuation ratios also appear stretched compared to historical levels, though I find the company fairly priced relative to its peers.

However, I believe the underlying growth potential and cash flow strength outweigh these concerns.

data.title: Snapchat Will Snap Out Of The Rut - Initiating With A Buy
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Snapchat (NYSE:SNAP) will report earnings on October 29th. I'm initiating it with a buy before earnings. Despite the soft revenue guidance for Q3, further exacerbating what I'd call shaky investor confidence with an over 18% dip after the fact, I think the market has priced in the negatives. With the stock hitting a 52-week low of $8.29 on August 7th, I think a rebound is due. The stock's relative strength index is at 40, and it's edging closer to oversold territory. All these parts align to make me believe the worst has already happened, and Snap should see some upside. The disclaimer that goes with my buy rating is that Snap isn't a stock for the faint-hearted. I see more upside in 2HFY24, as the stock's movement proved possible over the last year. If there's one thing Snap has proven year to date, it is its stock's ability to really snap up or plunge after earnings.

I believe Snap has a couple of growth catalysts working in its favor, two of which are making me a buy. The first is management's investments in the Direct response ad platform, which, I think, will show up on top-line in the short term, despite competition from Meta (META) and TikTok. The latter is facing the pressures of being banned in the U.S., and if that happens, I expect it would return some of the migrated users back to Snap.

The other reason why I'm a buy on the long-term is I expect heightened engagement as a result of the company's investments in their augmented reality platform or AR, along with their strides in AI since launching My AI last year and the recent partnership with Google's Gemini on Vertex AI. Thomas Kurian, CEO of Google Cloud, said that Snap was one early leader in the world that helped people communicate...:

now it is at the forefront of using generative AI to build agents that create new value for its community. Gemini models on Vertex AI are now powering millions of multimodal interactions in the Snapchat app every day that allow Snapchatters to better engage with My AI - with built-in accuracy and safety features.

Snap In A Snap: The Stock Performance

On the three-month chart, as seen below, the stock movement shows a negative market sentiment. The stock is down 28.2%, underperforming the S&P 500, which is up 6.3%. Its peer, Pinterest (PINS), is down 19%, but Meta (META) is up 20%, and Reddit (RDDT) is also up 21.4%. I think there is a limited downside from here and growth catalysts that haven't been priced in yet.

The year-to-date chart offers an illustration of a normal day for Snap's stock movement, which proved to be volatile. On the year-to-date chart, Snap is underperforming its peers and the S&P 500. The stock is down 7.4%, against the S&P 500, which is up 11.7%, Meta is up 13.2%, Reddit is up 55.7%, and Pinterest is down 3.7%.

As seen below, the stock plunged over 30% in early February on weak 1Q24 guidance due to the war in the Middle East. Fast-forward to late April, after 1Q24 results beat analysts' expectations, and revenue growth returned to double digits; the stock soared over 23% in extended trading. The last dip was that of two months ago, in early August, as the company reported 2Q24 earnings, and the stock dipped over 20% on weak 3Q24 guidance. With all said and done, I think the stock has an upside ahead after the company guides higher than investor expectations on the next earnings report with already low market expectations. I think that's going to happen for two main reasons, which I'll discuss in a second.

DR Ad Growth To The Rescue

DR advertising revenue increased 16% year-over-year in Q2, which is in line with the 17% in Q1, regardless of the tough comparison. The nature of brand businesses is very volatile, and marketing investments can be limited according to the ongoing economic situation. This can be seen in Q2 North America revenue that grew as little as 12% year-over-year due to the "impact of weaker brand-oriented demand" that's mainly concentrated in the region. Despite that, DR was a bright spot in the region, backed by strong growth in the SMB customer segment, which I'll get to in a second. Now, back to the tougher economic environment, it affected certain marketing investments in verticals where the company has had really good performance in direct response. An example of that is last quarter's restaurants vertical, which witnessed a tough environment in certain cases, but according to management, since DR is based on performance,

that's a category that continues to grow for us on the DR side in the current quarter because we've got great performance for those advertisers, and they've been able to continue to invest and grow their business on the back of that.

Due to the macro volatility I mentioned earlier, management is now more focused on investing to counter that volatility from all sides, and I'm optimistic about that. The company is seeing good momentum on its 7-0 Pixel Purchase Optimizations. On the advertisers' side, there has been a great benefit of the 7-0 optimization for app installation and app purchases, which showed good results during testing last quarter. CAP adoption is up 300% year-over-year, further indicating improved performance. That, along with the increase in total active advertisers to more than double year-over-year last quarter, should reflect in continued growth in DR advertising revenue in 2H24.

While we're on the SMB segment, management is focusing a big chunk of investments in the coming year on this segment. I share management's sentiment and think it "leverages the strength of our scale with our community and also our ability on the product and engineering side to drive results for advertisers." I think this will help with long-term growth and give the company a diversified revenue stream.

Valuation

Snap has a market cap of 17.41 billion and an enterprise value of 18.30 billion. Market sentiment on the stock is generally cautious, with over 72% of Street Analysts giving it a hold rating. Around 4.5% of Street analysts give the stock a strong buy, and ~16% give it a buy. Only 6.8% of analysts give the stock a sell.

The PT median and mean both notice a downward trend, reflecting investor caution and a dash of pessimism about the stock. The PT median was at $16 in late July and went down to $13 in August. The downward trajectory continued into September at $12 and is currently maintaining that. The mean PT was around $15 in July and was also down to $12.9 in August. It continued downwards to $12.6 in September, currently at $12.4. According to data from Refinitiv, Snap's EV/Sales (TTM) is 3.56, and its EV/Sales is 3.31, significantly lower than Meta's EV/Sales (TTM) at 9.7 and EV/Sales at 8.97.

Free cash flow was negative $73 million last quarter, down 38% from a year ago quarter, still reflecting the seasonality of lower revenue in Q1. I expect the numbers to improve now that headwinds from seasonality are behind us and management is focusing on balanced investments to sustain a positive cash flow. Net loss saw an improvement in Q2, at $249 million compared to the net loss of $377 million in a year ago quarter. I think the headcount reduction related to restructuring recently reflected positively on expenses. I believe such tactical reduction in expenses positions the company for continued profit down the line, accompanied by strategic investments. Speaking of which, for Q3 investment plans, management is prioritizing investments carefully to "deliver against the cost plans we have set out for our business, while investing prudently to deliver for our community and our partners."

What's Next: Annual Snap Partner Summit

AR

Last quarter, the number of users sharing AR Lens experiences increased by 12% year-over-year due to AR experience optimizations and AI Lenses' popularity. A clear indication is the ML Scribble World Lens, which was viewed over 1 billion times during the same quarter. At the annual partner summit event in mid-September, management revealed the fifth generation of its AR glasses, along with enhancements that add to its competitive edge. With creator-driven content taking a more significant chunk of attention for management, the number of creators "posting publicly has more than tripled over the past year," with over "15 billion interactions between creators and their fans." In his remarks, the company's CEO Evan Spiegel discussed VR sets in comparison to AR sets, in a way throwing shade at rivals Meta and Apple (AAPL). He said:

VR headsets…feel like you've got a laptop taped to your face, and they're isolating, they make people feel motion sick, they're heavy, they're uncomfortable. But AR glasses have see-through lenses. They allow you to share experiences together with your friends in the real world, and they're lightweight and wearable.

AI

At the summit, the company also introduced an AI-generated tool to creators in an effort to enhance its AI and machine learning capabilities for a better user experience. After unveiling My AI last year, a GPT-3 chatbot for Snapchat+ subscribers. According to Snapchat's vice president of products, Ceci Mourkogiannis, the new tool will help creators "make engaging videos with AI from a simple text, or soon, image prompt." I believe Snap is one step ahead of Meta and ByteDance's TikTok, which are yet to have their own AI-generated video capabilities.

data.title: AGCO Corporation - Improving Returns Came From Efficiency Improvements
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Over the past decade, AGCO Corporation (NYSE:AGCO) has managed to improve its returns. This came from improving operating margins, as reflected by better gross profit margins and lower SGA margins. This is an important edge as the agricultural machinery market is mature with a single-digit revenue growth rate. Profits growth will have to come from improving efficiencies.

The company is also financially sound and a good cash generator, which will mitigate the debt it took to acquire Trimble. I also found that there is more than a 30% margin of safety at the current market price. This is an investment opportunity.

Background

AGCO was founded in 1990 as a manufacturer and distributor of farm equipment in North America. The company has since grown to be “a global leader in the design, manufacture, and distribution of agricultural machinery and precision agriculture technology.”

  • In 2023, North America only accounted for about ¼ of the total revenue. You can see from the right part of Chart 1 that currently Europe/Middle is the biggest revenue contributor.
  • In terms of products, the company's main revenue came from tractors. In 2023, sales of tractors accounted for about 60% of the total revenue. Refer to the left part of Chart 1.

Note to Chart 1: The 2014 Europe/Middle East revenue included those from Africa.

Over the past few months, there were 2 important changes to the business direction:

  • In April 2024, the company completed the USD 2 billion joint venture with Trimble Inc. “to better serve farmers with factory fit and aftermarket applications in the mixed fleet precision agriculture market.”
  • In July 2024, the company “entered into a definitive agreement to sell the majority of its Grain & Protein business for USD 700 million.

The company stated that the sale of the Grain & Protein business “does not represent a strategic shift that will have a major effect on the consolidated results of operations.”

Nevertheless, taken together with the Trimble joint venture, I see this as a sign that the company will continue to focus on the agricultural machinery sector.

I do not see AGCO becoming a tech company but rather one that taps into digital tech to better serve the farmers. This is analogous to car companies using digital technology to enhance the user experience. They want to sell more cars. I do not think that car companies are going the way of Uber.

In this context, the global tractors and agricultural machinery market is a mature one with projected single-digit growth.

“The global agricultural tractors market demand was 3,200 thousand units in 2023… is expected to reach around 5,841 thousand units by 2033, expanding at a CAGR of 6.2% from 2024 to 2033.” Precedence Research

“The global agriculture tractor market size was valued at 2.05 million units in 2023 and is expected to reach 2.56 million units by 2029, growing at a CAGR of 3.76% during the forecast period” Arizton.

“Global demand for agricultural equipment is projected to advance 3.6% per year to $216 billion in 2028.” Freedonia Group

“The global demand for the agricultural equipment market is projected to increase at a CAGR of 4.2% during the forecast period between 2023 and 2033” Future Market Insights.

Apart from single-digit growth, there was also a significant increase in the Producer Price Index for agricultural machinery over the past few years. Refer to the left part of Chart 2.

Operating trends

Given the single-digit projected growth rates, you should not be surprised to find that over the past decade, AGCO’s revenue grew at 4.5% CAGR.

Given the single-digit growth in market demand, I would deduce that AGCO maintained its market share.

As can be seen from the left part of Chart 3, the bulk of the growth seemed to be from 2021 onwards. The company attributed this to “improved market demand and favorable pricing impacts.”

I think this is in line with the increase in the Producer Price Index shown in Chart 2. The result was a significant improvement in profits from 2020 to 2023. Refer to the left part of Chart 3.

The returns followed the profit performance as illustrated in the right part of Chart 3. You can see the significant jump from 2020 for the ROIC and ROE. Over the past decade,

  • ROIC averaged 12% compared to the current WACC of 8%.
  • ROE averaged 13% compared to the current cost of equity of 10%

With both these returns greater than the respective cost of funds, they suggest that shareholders’ value was created.

Efficiencies

Over the past 12 years, there was also an improvement in gross profitability (gross profits/total assets). This averaged 31% for 2022/23 compared to 26% for 2014/15. Refer to the left part of Chart 3.

In addition, there was also a better gross profit margin and a decline in the Selling, General, and Administration (SGA) margin. This demonstrates good cost control. There was also an improvement in the contribution margin. Refer to Chart 4.

Note to Op Profit Profile. I broke down the operating profits into fixed costs and variable costs.

  • Fixed cost = SGA, Depreciation & Amortization and Others.
  • Variable cost = Cost of Sales – Depreciation & Amortization.
  • Contribution = Revenue – Variable Cost.
  • Contribution margin = Contribution/Revenue.

To see whether the improvement in efficiency was broad-based, I looked at the performance of several operating and capital efficiencies metrics. I would conclude that while there was an improvement in the operating efficiency, I could not say the same thing for capital efficiency.

  • Chart 5, left part - Operating efficiency index. Except for inventory turnover that declined, all the other 3 metrics showed improvements.
  • Chart 5, right part - Capital efficiency index. There was only an improvement in the Reinvestment margin.

Peer comparison

I compared AGCO with several global agricultural machinery companies, as listed in Table 1. Apart from its recent top position in the return on capital, I would rate AGCO's performance as average compared to its peers.

You can see that AGCO is among the smaller ones in the peer group in terms of revenue. However, it delivered about average revenue growth from 2014 to 2023. I think this is consistent with my deduction that AGCO managed to maintain its market share over the past decade.

AGCO had an improving return on capital and has become the best among its peers since 2020. However, it was among the bottom performers when it came to EBIT margin. Refer to Chart 6.

In the context of the Levered Free Cash Flow Margin and EPS, I would rate AGCO's performance as average. Refer to Chart 7.

Financial position

I would rate AGCO financially sound based on the following.

From 2014 to 2023, it delivered positive cash flow from operations every year. During this period, it generated USD 6.7 billion cash flow from operations, compared to its USD 4.8 billion net income. This is a very good cash conversion ratio.

It had USD 657 million in cash and short-term investments as of the end of June 2024. This was about 5% of its total assets.

Over the past 12 years, it had a Reinvestment rate of 34%. Excluding the acquisitions, it had an average Reinvestment rate of 16%. Reinvestment rate = Reinvestment / NOPAT. This meant that a big part of the NOPAT could be returned to shareholders.

It had a good capital allocation plan as shown in Table 2. The cash flow from operations was enough to fund its CAPEX and acquisitions. Excess was returned to shareholders as dividends and share buybacks.

While it had a debt-capital ratio of 47% as of Jun 2024, this has increased from its pre-2024 high of 37% to fund the Trimble joint venture.

According to the Damodaran Jan 2024 data set, the debt-capital ratio for the machinery sector was 14%. I would not worry too much about AGCO's debt level given its historical cash generation ability as illustrated in Table 2.

Valuation

I adopted the following picture in valuing AGCO.

This is a mature company in a mature sector. I do not expect the Trimble joint venture to lead to significant revenue growth. In its announcement on the joint venture, Trimble stated that its “precision agriculture business…is expected to generate fiscal 2023 revenues of approximately $535 million.” In this context:

  • I will value AGCO based on a perpetual revenue growth rate of 4%.
  • I will use the company’s 2024 revenue outlook as the base revenue. This is 13% lower than the 2023 revenue.

Product prices were high over the past few years, boosting margins and efficiencies. On the conservative side, I will use the past 10 years' margin and efficiencies as the base.

On such a basis, I obtained an intrinsic value of USD 133 per share compared to its market price of USD 99 per share (22 Oct 2024). There is more than 30% margin of safety.

This margin of safety is supported by the Acquirer’s Multiple and Free Cash Flow yield, as shown in Table 3.

Notes to Table 3:

a) EV / (average 3 years Op profit + Depreciation).

b) 2014 to 2023 Average Free cash flow / market price.

Valuation model

I used a single-stage Free Cash Flow to the Firm (FCFF) model where the EBIT was determined based on the following:

FCFF = EBIT(1 – t) - Reinvestment

EBIT = Revenue X contribution margin – Fixed cost. This was based on the operating model as shown in the right part of Chart 4.

The Reinvestment was derived from the historical Reinvestment margin.

Value of firm = FCFF X (1+g) / (WACC – g).

Value of equity = Value of firm + cash + Other investments – Debt – Minority interests + excess Total capital employed

Table 4 illustrates the calculation.

The WACC was determined from the first page results of a Google search for “AGCO WACC” as illustrated in Table 5.

Risks and limitations

I think the key valuation risk relates to Chart 2. Product prices have been high over the past few years relative to pre-2020 by about 40% to 50%.

I am not sure whether the current prices will hold. In my valuation, I used the 2024 revenue projection by the company in its Q2 2024 presentation. This is lower than the 2023 revenue by about 13%. If this was further reduced by 6%, I would get the market price of the stock.

I do not have a crystal ball on how the long-term product price will be. To mitigate this, I carried out a Monte Carlo simulation of the valuation that focus on the revenue downside. The simulation used random samples of the following variables, assuming that they follow a triangular distribution.

  • Revenue. The revenue would range from the base value to 20% lower.
  • Efficiencies. The contribution margin, capital turnover, and Reinvestment margin would range from – 10% to + 10% of the base values.
  • WACC. This would range from 7% to 8.8% as per the range shown in Table 5.

The results based on 2,000 runs are shown in Chart 8. There is a 50% chance that the intrinsic value would be higher than the market price. Note that this is an analysis skewed towards lower product prices.

In other words, assuming that revenue in the long run would never be higher than the Jun 2024 projection, there is a 1-to-1 odds that the intrinsic value is greater than the market price. I would have thought that this is a good downside protection picture.

Conclusion

I would consider AGCO fundamentally sound.

  • It maintained its market share over the past decade. Its joint venture with Trimble will ensure that it can enhance the solutions it provides to its customers.
  • It managed to improve its profits, which in turn led to improving returns. It had the best returns over the past few years compared to its global peers.
  • It is financially sound with a good cash generation track record.
  • There are signs of improving operating efficiencies, although there is some doubt about improving capital efficiencies.

A conservative valuation shows that there is more than a 30% margin of safety. Furthermore, a Monte Carlo simulation that focuses on revenue downside shows a 1-to-1 odds that the intrinsic value would be greater than the market price.

You can understand why I see this as an investment opportunity.

data.title: Tesla, Inc. 2024 Q3 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Tesla, Inc. in conjunction with their 2024 Q3 earnings call.
